infobox.value: or **location indicator** is a four-letter [code](/wiki/Code "Code") designating [aerodromes](/wiki/Aerodrome "Aerodrome") around the world. These codes, as defined by the [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization") and published quarterly in ICAO Document 7910: *Location Indicators*, are used by [air traffic control](/wiki/Air_traffic_control "Air traffic control") and airline operations such as [flight planning](/wiki/Flight_planning "Flight planning").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Four-letter code designation for aviation facilities around the world

For a list of airports by ICAO code, see [Lists of airports § By ICAO code](/wiki/Lists_of_airports#By_ICAO_code "Lists of airports").

Not to be confused with [IATA airport code](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/ICAO_FirstLetter.svg/250px-ICAO_FirstLetter.svg.png)](/wiki/File:ICAO_FirstLetter.svg)

Map of the world's ICAO classifications according to the first letter of its ICAO airport code

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/ICAO-countries.png/250px-ICAO-countries.png)](/wiki/File:ICAO-countries.png)

Map of countries classified with ICAO airport code prefixes and subnational regions with their respective second ICAO letter

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Flag_of_ICAO.svg/250px-Flag_of_ICAO.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_ICAO.svg)

Flag of the ICAO

The **ICAO airport code** or **location indicator** is a four-letter [code](/wiki/Code "Code") designating [aerodromes](/wiki/Aerodrome "Aerodrome") around the world. These codes, as defined by the [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization") and published quarterly in ICAO Document 7910: *Location Indicators*, are used by [air traffic control](/wiki/Air_traffic_control "Air traffic control") and airline operations such as [flight planning](/wiki/Flight_planning "Flight planning").
ICAO codes are also used to identify other aviation facilities such as [weather stations](/wiki/Weather_station "Weather station"), international [flight service stations](/wiki/Flight_service_station "Flight service station"), or [area control centers](/wiki/Area_control_center "Area control center") (and by extension their [flight information regions](/wiki/Flight_information_region "Flight information region")), regardless of whether they are located at airports.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ICAO_airport_code&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The recommendations for ICAO airport codes were adopted on 24 March 1959, and came into force on 1 October the same year.[[1]](#cite_note-icao-ais-201807-1)

## ICAO codes versus IATA codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ICAO_airport_code&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: ICAO codes versus IATA codes")]

ICAO codes are separate and different from the three-letter [IATA codes](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"), which are generally used for [airline timetables](/wiki/Airline_timetable "Airline timetable"), reservations, and [baggage](/wiki/Baggage "Baggage") tags. For example, the IATA code for [London](/wiki/London "London")'s [Heathrow Airport](/wiki/London_Heathrow_Airport "London Heathrow Airport") is LHR and its ICAO code is EGLL.

In general IATA codes are usually derived from the name of the airport or the city it serves, while ICAO codes are distributed by region and country. Far more [aerodromes](/wiki/Aerodrome "Aerodrome") (in the broad sense) have ICAO codes than IATA codes, which are sometimes assigned to [railway stations](/wiki/Train_station "Train station") as well. The selection of ICAO codes is partly delegated to authorities in each country, while IATA codes, which have no geographic structure, must be decided centrally by IATA.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ICAO_airport_code&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Structure")]

The first one or two letters of the ICAO code indicate the country or large region of a country; the remaining letters identify the airport. For example, the ICAO code for [Heathrow International Airport](/wiki/Heathrow_International_Airport "Heathrow International Airport") in London, is EGLL, with EG reflecting that it is based in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). By contrast, IATA codes do not provide geographic reference. For example, LHR, representing Heathrow, does not enable one to deduce the location of the airport LHV with any greater certainty; it is [William T. Piper Memorial Airport](/wiki/William_T._Piper_Memorial_Airport "William T. Piper Memorial Airport") in [Lock Haven, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Lock_Haven,_Pennsylvania "Lock Haven, Pennsylvania") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

There are a few exceptions to the regional structure of the ICAO code for political or administrative reasons:

* [RAF Mount Pleasant](/wiki/RAF_Mount_Pleasant "RAF Mount Pleasant") air base in the [Falkland Islands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands"), for instance, is assigned the ICAO code EGYP as though it were in the United Kingdom, but nearby civilian [Port Stanley Airport](/wiki/Port_Stanley_Airport "Port Stanley Airport") is assigned SFAL, consistent with [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America").
* [Saint Pierre and Miquelon](/wiki/Saint_Pierre_and_Miquelon "Saint Pierre and Miquelon") is controlled by France, and airports there are assigned LFxx as though they were in Europe.
* [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") is assigned the code BKxx grouping it with [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") and [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") rather than its geographical neighbors which have Lxxx (described below).
* [Jerusalem International Airport](/wiki/Jerusalem_International_Airport "Jerusalem International Airport") was assigned both LLJR (its Israeli persona) as well as OJJR (its Jordanian persona), but the airport itself fell into disuse.

In the contiguous United States and Canada, many airports have ICAO codes that are simply copies of their three-letter [IATA](/wiki/IATA "IATA") codes, with the geographical prefix added on (e.g., YEG and CYEG both refer to [Edmonton International Airport](/wiki/Edmonton_International_Airport "Edmonton International Airport"), while IAD and KIAD both refer to [Washington Dulles International Airport](/wiki/Washington_Dulles_International_Airport "Washington Dulles International Airport")). This similarity does not extend to [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") (PAxx), [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") (PHxx), or U.S. territories. [Kahului Airport](/wiki/Kahului_Airport "Kahului Airport") on [Maui](/wiki/Maui "Maui"), for instance, has an IATA code of OGG and an ICAO code of PHOG.

ICAO airport codes do not begin with I, J, X, or Q, though the [Jezero Crater](/wiki/Jezero_(crater) "Jezero (crater)") on [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") is assigned the special ICAO code JZRO, and some have used this to deduce that J must be the code for Mars, even though it has never been officially reserved by ICAO.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Codes beginning with I (Ixx and Ixxx) are often used for [navigational aids](/wiki/Navigational_aid "Navigational aid") such as radio beacons, while the [Q code](/wiki/Q_code "Q code") is reserved for international radiocommunications and non-geographical special use.

In [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), the Latin letter X, or its [Morse](/wiki/Morse_Code "Morse Code")/[Baudot](/wiki/ITA2 "ITA2") Cyrillic equivalent [Ь](/wiki/%D0%AC "Ь"), are used to designate government, military, and experimental aviation airfields in internal airfield codes similar in structure and purpose to ICAO codes but not used internationally.[[3]](#cite_note-3) ZZZZ is a pseudo-code, used in [flight plans](/wiki/Flight_plan "Flight plan") for aerodromes with no ICAO code assigned.

ICAO codes are sometimes updated. [Johannesburg Airport](/wiki/Johannesburg_Airport "Johannesburg Airport") in [Johannesburg](/wiki/Johannesburg "Johannesburg"), South Africa, for instance, was formerly known as Jan Smuts International Airport, with code FAJS. When the airport was renamed O. R. Tambo International Airport, its ICAO code was updated to FAOR.

Some airports have two ICAO codes, usually when an airport is shared by civilian and military users. [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport") in [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), Germany, for instance, has been assigned ICAO code EDDF while [Rhein-Main Air Base](/wiki/Rhein-Main_Air_Base "Rhein-Main Air Base") was assigned ICAO code EDAF until its closure. [Sion Airport](/wiki/Sion_Airport "Sion Airport") in Switzerland has code LSGS while its military facilities have the ICAO code LSMS. [Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport") in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), Belgium, has the ICAO code EBBR for its civilian facilities, and [Melsbroek Air Base](/wiki/Melsbroek_Air_Base "Melsbroek Air Base") has been assigned ICAO code EBMB, even though the two airports share runways and ground and air control facilities.

## Pseudo ICAO-codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ICAO_airport_code&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pseudo ICAO-codes")]

In small countries like Belgium or the Netherlands, almost all aerodromes have an ICAO code. For larger countries like the UK or Germany this is not feasible, given the limited number of letter codes. Some countries have addressed this issue by introducing a scheme of sub-ICAO aerodrome codes; France, for example, assigns pseudo ICAO codes in the style *LFddnn*, where *dd* indicates the [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") while *nn* is a sequential counter. The French Federation of Ultralight Motorized Gliders was formally named the keeper of these codes. Aerodrome de Torreilles in France, for instance, has code LF6651.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In [Antarctica many aerodromes](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Antarctica "List of airports in Antarctica") have pseudo ICAO-codes with AT and two digits, while others have proper codes from countries performing air control such as NZ for [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").
